sentences.base_language: Today I want to cook at home.
sentences.target_language: 今日は家で料理したいです。
sentences.romanized: Kyō wa ie de ryōri shitai desu.
sentences.grammarNote: Time words like 今日 (today), 明日 (tomorrow), and 毎日 (every day) come at the start of the sentence in Japanese — or right after the topic marker. Unlike English, they don't go to the end.

sentences.base_language: First, I go to buy vegetables.
sentences.target_language: まず、野菜を買いに行きます。
sentences.romanized: Mazu, yasai wo kai ni ikimasu.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜に行く (ni iku) expresses purpose of movement: 'go in order to do…'. The purpose verb takes the stem + に: 買いに行く (go to buy), 食べに行く (go to eat). This is the standard Japanese pattern for purposeful outings.

sentences.base_language: I need two tomatoes and some garlic.
sentences.target_language: トマトが二つとにんにくが少し必要です。
sentences.romanized: Tomato ga futatsu to ninniku ga sukoshi hitsuyō desu.
sentences.literal: Tomatoes two and garlic some need.
sentences.grammarNote: Japanese has two counter systems: native Japanese (一つ、二つ、三つ…) and Sino-Japanese (一個、二個…). For small generic items, 一つ/二つ (hitotsu/futatsu) are often used. 必要 (hitsuyō) = 'necessary / needed'.

sentences.target_language: それから野菜を洗います。

sentences.base_language: I put the oil in the pan.
sentences.target_language: フライパンに油を入れます。
sentences.grammarNote: に (ni) marks the destination or target for an action: フライパンに入れる (put into the pan), お皿に盛る (serve onto a plate). Japanese uses に for both direction of movement and the target of an action like placing or giving.
匂い (nioi) = 'smell / aroma'. Japanese uses いい匂い (good smell) as a noun phrase rather than a verb 'smells good'. Compare with 〜そうだ (looks/seems like): おいしそうな匂い = 'a smell that seems delicious'.
できる (dekiru) in this context means 'to be done / completed / ready'. 料理ができた = 'the food is ready'. This is different from できる meaning 'can do': both uses are very common in Japanese.
